title: Kozo washi sheet forming
subtitle: The nagashizuki technique for forming Japanese washi from mulberry fibre
excerpt: Washi made from kozo (paper mulberry) fibre differs from Western paper in how the sheet is formed: where Western papermakers pull and hold a mould level to let water drain, the Japanese nagashizuki technique involves repeated forward and sideways rocking motions while the pulp is still flowing. The result is an interlocked long-fibre sheet with exceptional strength and a characteristic surface texture.
sourceNotes: Timothy Barrett, Japanese Papermaking: Traditions, Tools, and Techniques (1983), Weatherhill. Barrett's documentation of the nagashizuki method is the standard English-language reference. The foundational process is also covered in Sukey Hughes, Washi: The World of Japanese Paper (1978). Both are in the public domain or treated as such for the technique description drawn here.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Japanese sheet-forming technique involving repeated rocking of the mould to interlace fibres in multiple directions. The word means approximately 'flowing formation'.
The paper mulberry (Broussonetia papyrifera), whose inner bark fibres are the primary raw material for traditional Japanese washi. Kozo fibres are very long and produce strong, flexible sheets.
A natural mucilage extracted from the root of the tororo-aoi plant (or produced from other sources), added to the washi vat to slow fibre drainage and allow repeated rocking during sheet formation.
The Western sheet-forming approach (raise and hold level while water drains). Contrasted with nagashizuki, in which the mould is actively rocked during formation.

content.text: The defining difference between washi and Western paper is the
forming, the mould is lifted and held still while water drains. In nagashizuki, the mould is rocked forward and back, then side to side, repeatedly, while the
fibres are still in motion. This interlocks the long fibres in crossing directions, producing a sheet that is remarkably strong relative to its weight. The rocking is enabled by
, a mucilaginous formation agent that keeps the fibres suspended longer than plain water would. Without neri, the fibres would settle and drain before the rocking sequence could interlace them.

items.name: Kozo half-stuff or prepared kozo pulp (from specialist papermaking suppliers)
Neri (tororo-aoi mucilage or synthetic substitute, sold as powdered or liquid formation aid)
Mould and deckle (a flexible keta-style mould is traditional, but a rigid Western mould can be used for practice)
Large vat, at least 60 litres for a half A3 sheet

content.text: Beat or blend the kozo half-stuff gently. Kozo fibres should remain long: 10 to 15 seconds in a kitchen blender per small batch is sufficient. Over-beating shortens the fibres and destroys the strength advantage of kozo.

Add the beaten kozo to the vat with water to a concentration slightly lower than for cotton linter. Add the neri and stir gently. The neri makes the water slightly viscous; it should feel slippery but not thick. Test by running a sheet of water off your hand: with neri correctly dosed, the water clings for a moment before running.
Stir the vat, then lower the mould into the far end at 45 degrees and draw it toward you to horizontal in the normal Western pull. Do not lift out yet.

While the fibres are still in suspension on the mould surface, rock the mould gently away from you (the front edge lifts slightly) and back toward you, twice. Then rock the mould gently left and right, twice. The pulp slurry ripples across the surface; this motion interlaces the long kozo fibres.

On the third or fourth rock, tilt the mould to pour the excess slurry back into the vat from the far edge. This deposits the final layer of fibres. Hold the mould level and let the water drain through the neri-slowed vat.

Drain is slow: 1 to 2 minutes before the sheet is ready to couch. Couch using the standard rolling method onto a couching cloth. Stack sheets without additional cloths between them if forming a traditional post (washi sheets couch directly onto each other and separate after pressing).

attrs.heading: Common problems with kozo washi
attrs.intro: Problems that appear in the first attempts at nagashizuki forming.

items.symptom: Sheet drains too quickly to complete the rocking sequence
items.cause: Insufficient neri, or neri not properly dissolved.
items.fix: Add more neri to the vat. Stir gently to distribute. Re-test drainage rate before forming.

items.symptom: Sheet is weak and tears during couching
items.cause: Fibres too short from over-beating, or rocking motion too vigorous causing fibre alignment in one direction only.
items.fix: Reduce blending time. Rock more gently and evenly in both axes.
